---
title: "Inputs"
---

Most of zen-do's functionality takes one or more of these main inputs:

## `.zenodo.toml` metadata file

This file contains the metadata for the deposit. It should represent the
"source of truth", as any action taken by zen-do when interacting with
the Zenodo API will use this file and overwrite anything in the Zenodo
deposit. Users can't directly give zen-do this file, as any
functionality that depends on it will look for the file in the current
working directory.

::: callout-note
For the `convert` command, the input is actually a format other than the
`.zenodo.toml` file, such as the `.zenodo.json` file. But the same or
similar metadata is contained in those other formats.
:::

### Unique ID between metadata and deposit

The way we envision how we use zen-do depends on being able to determine
whether a given metadata file has an existing Zenodo deposit on their
servers or not. However, if a metadata file doesn't have a connected
Zenodo deposit on their servers, there is no way for Zenodo (or us) to
uniquely determine whether a deposit exists or not.

While Zenodo will create a unique deposit ID once a deposit has been
created, that means that the user (or us) would have to create the
deposit first and then add the deposit ID to the metadata file. This is
not how we want zen-do to behave.

Likewise, without a unique identifier, we would have to compare the
contents of the metadata file with all the metadata contents from each
deposit returned from Zenodo. We couldn't rely on searching for an
identifier within `related_identifiers` using, for example, the GitHub
repository URL or something similar identifier because we also want to
be able to create multiple deposits for the same repository, e.g. one
for the source code and one for the documentation.

So, given the way Zenodo works and how we want zen-do to be used, we
need some way to uniquely identify the metadata to accurately determine
whether a deposit exists or not. We decided on using a URN ID that looks
like:

```text
urn:zenodo:<unique-id>:<optional-sub-id>
```

The only required parts are the `urn:zenodo` prefix and the
`<unique-id>`. But if the user wanted to have multiple deposits for the
same `<unique-id>`, they should use the optional `<optional-sub-id>` to
distinguish between those outputs. If the metadata file is hosted on a
GitHub repository, we recommend:

```text
urn:zenodo:<github-username>:<repo-name>:<output-type>
```

So if we made a metadata file for the zen-do repository to upload the
documentation as a PDF as a deposit, it might look like
`urn:zenodo:seedcase-project:zen-do:docs`. If we wanted to make a
deposit for the same repository but for the source code instead, it
might look like `urn:zenodo:seedcase-project:zen-do:source`. The final
`<output_type>` part is used to distinguish between different types of
deposits for the same repository and are arbitrary and up to the user to
decide on the name.

Because we depend on this URN, all metadata files must have this URN in
the `related_identifiers` field in the `.zenodo.toml` file. This also
means that the user needs to manually add it and manually decide on an
ID that is unique enough for their purposes.

## Zenodo access token

This is required in order to interact with the Zenodo API. Like the
`.zenodo.toml` file, users also can't directly give zen-do this token.
For security purposes, zen-do doesn't accept the token as an argument
since that would be visible in the terminal history and process list.
Instead, zen-do will look for a token with the name `ZENODO_TOKEN` (or
`ZENODO_SANDBOX_TOKEN`) in the system keyring first and, if not found,
in the environment variables.

We only allow the use of an environment variable because continuous
deployment platforms like GitHub Actions have built-in support for
secret environment variables, and this is a common way to store and use
secrets in these type of pipelines. Outside of these deployment
platforms, we recommend users to store their token in their system
keyring, which is a more secure way to store secrets.

## Files

The only files users can directly give to zen-do are files that make up
the content of the Zenodo deposition. These files will be be uploaded
along with the metadata in `.zenodo.toml` to the Zenodo deposit. Any
file is accepted that is uploadable to Zenodo.
